content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 5, 1990


Mr. Wes Van Winkle
BAGATELOS & FADEM
The International Building
601 California Street, Suite 1801
San Francisco, CA 94108

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-635

Dear Mr. Winkle:

This is in response to your letter dated October 8, 1990
seeking confirmation of telephone advice given to you by Wayne
Imberi of our office on October 5, 1990. The advice concerns your
client, Taxpayers for Common Sense.

In your letter, you seek confirmation that the "sponsored
committee" identification requirements of the Political Reform Act
("the Act")¹, specifically the requirements of Section 84106, do
not apply to broadcast advertisements. We confirm that advice.

Your letter also makes reference to the Truth in Initiative
Advertising provisions of the Act (Sections 84504, et seq.).
These provisions require that, in certain cases, initiative
advertisements identify their major funding sources. You indicate
in the letter that your client intends to begin broadcasting
advertisements that both support Proposition 126 and oppose
Proposition 134, an initiative placed on the ballot after its
proponents obtained a sufficient number of qualifying signatures.
Proposition 126 is a measure placed on the ballot by the
Legislature. You further indicate that you believe that the Truth
in Initiative Advertising provisions of the Act do not require
your client to identify the major funding sources for these
advertisements if not more than fifty percent of any advertisement
is devoted to one those initiatives.


_____________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

As we explained to you on October 25, 1990, we cannot confirm
that your client is exempt from the Truth in Initiative Advertising requirements of the Act for its advertisements which both support Proposition 126 and oppose Proposition 134.

As we explained, we believe that the provision on which you
base your claim of exemption (Section 84512) is an unintended
"loophole" which, if exploited, undermines the clear disclosure
requirements in broadcast advertisements. Consequently, we feel
we must construe the Truth in Initiative Advertising law, whenever
possible, to favor the required disclosure. This is in accordance
with Section 81003 of the Act, which requires liberal construction
of these provisions to accomplish their purpose. (See also,
Watson v. FPPC (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1059, 1076.)    Furthermore,
settled rules of statutory construction require that exceptions
such as that created by Section 84512 be construed narrowly.
(See, City and County of San Francisco v. Farrell (1982) 32 Cal.3d
47, 52.)

In applying this construction to the Act's Truth in Initiative Advertising provisions, we conclude that the "fifty percent
rule" set forth in Section 84512 does not exempt your client from
identifying its major funding sources in these advertisements.
Section 84501 makes clear that the disclosure requirements of the
Truth in Initiative Advertising law apply only to qualified statewide initiative measures and qualified statewide referendum measures. In our Hiltscher Advice Letter (No. A-90-186, copy enclosed), we advised that a measure placed on the ballot by the
Legislature was not a "qualified statewide initiative measure" and
thus, not subject to the requirements of the Truth in Initiative
Advertising law.

Consistent with this advice, and with our duty to construe
the Truth in Initiative Advertising law to require disclosure
whenever possible, we view the advertisement in this case to apply
only to one initiative - Proposition 134 - for purposes of Section
84512. As such, the disclosure requirements of the Truth in Initiative Advertising law are applicable to the advertisement.

Proposition 126, placed on the ballot by the Legislature, is
not an initiative either for purposes of the Act generally, or for
purposes of the Truth in Initiative Advertising provisions
specifically. The proposition is therefore irrelevant for
purposes of determining whether the advertisement in question is
"more than fifty percent devoted to one initiative" as prescribed
by Section 84512, because Section 84512 and the other Truth in
Initiative Advertising provisions are applicable to initiatives
only.

Therefore, for purposes of the Truth in Initiative
Advertising law, the advertisement in question devotes more than
fifty percent of its duration to the "passage or defeat of one
_________________________
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

initiative" because only the initiative referenced in these
advertisements is considered for purposes of Section 84512. An
exception from the Truth in Initiative Advertising provisions of
the Act permitting your client to avoid identifying the major
funding sources in an advertisement would be available only if two
or more initiatives were featured in the advertisement, and then
presented in such a manner so as not to have more than fifty
percent of the advertisement devoted to one initiative. Because
only one initiative is featured in the advertisements in question,
the exception provided in Section 84512 does not apply.

Finally, as we explained to you on October 25, 1990, because
the Truth in Initiative Advertising provisions of the Act require
your client to identify the major funding sources for the
advertisements in question, the advertisement must be changed to
reflect the proper disclosure required.

Should you have any questions, please contact me at (916) 322-
5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

SH:JR:ken

Enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: Does the 'sponsored committee' identification requirement under Political Reform Act Section 84106 apply to broadcast advertisements, and are the Truth in Initiative Advertising provisions (Sections 84501 et seq.) applicable to advertisements supporting Proposition 126 (legislatively-placed measure) and opposing Proposition 134 (initiative) when less than fifty percent of the advertisement is devoted to one initiative?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that Section 84106 sponsored committee identification requirements do not apply to broadcast advertisements. However, the Truth in Initiative Advertising disclosure requirements do apply to the client's advertisements because only Proposition 134 (a qualified statewide initiative) qualifies for purposes of Section 84512's fifty percent exemption; Proposition 126, being legislatively-placed, is not an 'initiative' under the Act and therefore is irrelevant to the fifty percent calculation, meaning the advertisements exceed fifty percent devoted to one qualifying initiative and must include major funding source disclosures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document has implicit question/conclusion structure rather than explicit headers. Telephone advice from October 5, 1990 was being confirmed in writing. Letter references prior Hiltachk Advice Letter No. A-90-185 for consistency. Some OCR errors present in original text (e.g., 'rrsponsored' instead of 'sponsored') but meaning is clear.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the 'sponsored committee' identification requirement under Political Reform Act Section 84106 apply to broadcast advertisements, and are the Truth in Initiative Advertising provisions (Sections 84501 et seq.) applicable to advertisements supporting Proposition 126 (legislatively-placed measure) and opposing Proposition 134 (initiative) when less than fifty percent of the advertisement is devoted to one initiative?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC confirms that Section 84106 sponsored committee identification requirements do not apply to broadcast advertisements. However, the Truth in Initiative Advertising disclosure requirements do apply to the client's advertisements because only Proposition 134 (a qualified statewide initiative) qualifies for purposes of Section 84512's fifty percent exemption; Proposition 126, being legislatively-placed, is not an 'initiative' under the Act and therefore is irrelevant to the fifty percent calculation, meaning the advertisements exceed fifty percent devoted to one qualifying initiative and must include major funding source disclosures.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms that Section 84106 sponsored committee identification requirements do not apply to broadcast advertisements. FPPC denies exemption from Truth in Initiative Advertising disclosure requirements, concluding that only Proposition 134 (an initiative) counts toward the fifty percent threshold under Section 84512, while Proposition 126 (legislatively-placed) does not, therefore requiring major funding source identification in the advertisements.
